questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a syncopal episode while at rest. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. His father died suddenly at age 40. ECG shows a coved-type ST elevation in leads V1-V2 with a downsloping ST segment followed by a negative T wave. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The coved ST elevation in V1-V2 is diagnostic of type 1 Brugada pattern. Family history of sudden death is a risk factor. Long QT syndrome has prolonged QT interval, ARVC has epsilon waves, HCM has LV hypertrophy, and vasovagal syncope typically occurs with standing.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man is admitted with a high fever (39.5°C) due to community-acquired pneumonia. He has no cardiac history. A routine ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1 and V2 with a saddle-back appearance. The QT interval is normal. Troponin is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fever can unmask Brugada syndrome. The saddle-back pattern is type 2 Brugada. Pericarditis typically has diffuse ST elevation and PR depression; myocarditis would elevate troponin; PE shows S1Q3T3; early repolarisation usually has notching and is not confined to V1-V2.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after collapsing at home. He was resuscitated from ventricular fibrillation. Post-resuscitation ECG shows a coved ST elevation in V1-V2 with a downsloping ST segment. Coronary angiography shows normal coronary arteries. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Normal coronary arteries exclude acute coronary syndrome. The ECG pattern is classic for Brugada syndrome. CPVT typically occurs with exercise and has bidirectional VT; long QT syndrome has prolonged QT; electrolyte imbalance would be evident on blood tests.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known Brugada syndrome and an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) presents after receiving two shocks. He had palpitations preceding the shocks, but no syncope. ICD interrogation shows ventricular tachycardia at 200 bpm that was successfully terminated. He is on no antiarrhythmics. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Appropriate ICD therapy for ventricular arrhythmia due to Brugada syndrome
Inappropriate shock due to atrial fibrillation
questions.explanation: The shocks were preceded by palpitations and the documented arrhythmia was ventricular tachycardia, indicating appropriate therapy for a Brugada-related arrhythmia. Atrial fibrillation would show atrial tachyarrhythmia, lead fracture causes oversensing, T-wave oversensing is a technical issue, and device infection would present with local/systemic signs.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old asymptomatic woman presents for a routine check-up. She is a first-degree relative of a patient with Brugada syndrome. Her ECG shows a saddle-back ST elevation of 2 mm in V1 and V2. The ST segment is not coved. There is no family history of sudden death in her immediate family. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The saddle-back pattern in leads V1-V2 is type 2 Brugada pattern, which in a first-degree relative strongly suggests Brugada syndrome. Benign early repolarisation occurs in inferior/lateral leads; RBBB has wide QRS with rsR' in V1; athlete's heart causes LV changes; ARVC has epsilon waves and T-wave inversions.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a single episode of syncope. His ECG shows a type 1 Brugada pattern (coved ST elevation ≥2 mm) in leads V1-V2. He is asymptomatic now. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of Brugada syndrome?
questions.explanation: A type 1 Brugada pattern on ECG is diagnostic of Brugada syndrome in the presence of clinical criteria (e.g., syncope). No further tests are needed to confirm the diagnosis. Ajmaline challenge is reserved for cases with non-diagnostic ECG.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with palpitations and has a family history of sudden cardiac death. Her resting ECG is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic test to unmask Brugada syndrome?
questions.explanation: A sodium channel blocker challenge (ajmaline or flecainide) is the gold standard to unmask a type 1 Brugada pattern in patients with suspected Brugada syndrome but a non-diagnostic baseline ECG. Holter and exercise tests are not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a known type 2 Brugada pattern undergoes an ajmaline challenge. During the test, no coved ST elevation develops. What is the correct interpretation of this result?
options: The patient has a low-risk variant of Brugada syndrome
The patient does not have Brugada syndrome
Genetic testing for SCN5A mutation is now indicated
The test is indeterminate and should be repeated
The patient requires prophylactic ICD insertion
questions.explanation: A negative sodium channel blocker challenge (no conversion to type 1 pattern) effectively excludes Brugada syndrome. False negatives are rare. Genetic testing is not first-line; ICD is treatment, not diagnostic interpretation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old asymptomatic woman with a family history of Brugada syndrome has a resting ECG showing a type 2 (saddleback) pattern in V2. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm or exclude Brugada syndrome?
options: Repeat ECG with high intercostal leads
questions.explanation: A type 2 pattern is not diagnostic; a drug challenge with ajmaline or flecainide is required to see if type 1 emerges. High leads may aid but are not definitive. Genetic testing is not diagnostic by itself.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man has been diagnosed with Brugada syndrome after a cardiac arrest. A type 1 pattern was documented. He has no other affected family members known. What is the most appropriate investigation to guide family screening?
options: Genetic testing for the patient to identify the causative mutation
Ajmaline challenge for all first-degree relatives
questions.explanation: Genetic testing for SCN5A mutation in the index patient allows cascade screening in first-degree relatives. EPS is for risk stratification, not family screening. Drug challenge is used if genetic testing is negative.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old asymptomatic man is incidentally found to have a spontaneous type 1 Brugada ECG pattern. He has no family history of sudden cardiac death. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Lifestyle modification including avoidance of fevers and sodium channel blockers
Electrophysiology study with programmed ventricular stimulation
questions.explanation: In asymptomatic low-risk Brugada (no syncope, no family history of SCD), NICE advises lifestyle modification—avoiding triggers like fever and sodium channel blockers—rather than ICD, quinidine, or routine EPS. ICD is reserved for high-risk patients.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known Brugada syndrome is successfully resuscitated after an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest due to ventricular fibrillation. He has no other comorbidities. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
options: Catheter ablation of the right ventricular outflow tract
questions.explanation: For survivors of cardiac arrest in Brugada syndrome, ICD implantation is the definitive therapy for secondary prevention (NICE and ESC guidelines). Quinidine or isoproterenol are adjunctive, not first-line definitive, and amiodarone is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with Brugada syndrome presents with multiple episodes of ventricular fibrillation over two hours (electrical storm). He is haemodynamically unstable. Which of the following is the most appropriate acute treatment?
questions.explanation: In Brugada electrical storm, intravenous isoproterenol is the drug of choice as it augments the calcium current and suppresses VF. Sodium channel blockers (procainamide, lidocaine) are contraindicated, and amiodarone is less effective acutely.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known Brugada syndrome (spontaneous type 1 ECG) is 12 weeks pregnant. She is asymptomatic. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: Flecainide and other class Ic sodium channel blockers are contraindicated in Brugada syndrome because they can unmask the ECG pattern and provoke ventricular arrhythmias. Quinidine, amiodarone, beta-blockers, and verapamil are relatively safe.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with Brugada syndrome presents with symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. He is in sinus rhythm at consultation. Which of the following is the most appropriate antiarrhythmic drug for rhythm control?
questions.explanation: In Brugada syndrome, quinidine is the preferred rhythm control drug for atrial fibrillation as it blocks the transient outward current and has anti-fibrillatory properties without the proarrhythmic risk of sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone) or sotalol. Amiodarone is an alternative but quinidine is first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with pneumonia. Routine ECG shows a type 1 Brugada pattern (coved ST elevation and T wave inversion in V1-V2). He is asymptomatic, with no history of syncope, palpitations, or sudden cardiac death in the family. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to cardiology for specialist assessment
Check serum sodium and potassium levels
Repeat ECG with electrodes placed in higher intercostal spaces
questions.explanation: An incidental type 1 Brugada pattern in an asymptomatic patient without family history requires specialist cardiology referral for risk stratification and possible provocation testing. Starting quinidine or checking electrolytes is not indicated here.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known Brugada syndrome is brought to the ED after collapsing at home. On arrival, he is unconscious, pulseless, and the monitor shows polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Deliver a single unsynchronised shock at 200J
Start chest compressions and defibrillate after 2 minutes
questions.explanation: For pulseless polymorphic VT (equivalent to VF), immediate defibrillation with an unsynchronised shock is the priority. Amiodarone is given after the third shock. Chest compressions should be started immediately but defibrillation should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with no cardiac history is found to have a type 2 Brugada pattern (saddleback ST elevation) on a routine ECG. Her father died suddenly at age 40. She is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange genetic testing for SCN5A mutation
questions.explanation: In patients with type 2 Brugada pattern and a family history of sudden death, an ajmaline (or flecainide) provocation test is indicated to unmask the type 1 pattern and confirm the diagnosis. Genetic testing is not first-line, and ICD implantation is not indicated without a definitive diagnosis or symptoms.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with Brugada syndrome is on quinidine for suppression of ventricular ectopy. He presents with palpitations and dizziness. ECG shows prolonged QTc (540 ms) and frequent ventricular ectopics. What is the most appropriate next action?
options: Increase quinidine dose to improve ectopy suppression
Start amiodarone as a safer alternative
Stop quinidine and check serum electrolytes
questions.explanation: Quinidine can cause QT prolongation, which predisposes to torsade de pointes. The first step is to discontinue the offending drug and correct any electrolyte abnormalities. Adding another antiarrhythmic may worsen the QT prolongation.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy collapses after running in the school playground. He rapidly recovers. ECG shows a type 1 Brugada pattern. His brother died suddenly at age 20. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to an inherited cardiac conditions service
questions.explanation: In a young patient with syncope and a type 1 pattern with family history, immediate referral to a specialist inherited cardiac conditions service is essential for comprehensive risk stratification and management. ICD may be indicated but should not be placed urgently without specialist input. Isoprenaline is used in electrical storms, not as primary treatment.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department after collapsing while watching television. He is now conscious but reports no warning symptoms beforehand. His ECG shows a type 1 Brugada pattern (coved ST elevation in V1-V2). Which finding in the history most strongly indicates that this patient is at high risk of sudden cardiac death?
options: Family history of sudden cardiac death
questions.explanation: Syncope without prodrome is a major risk factor for sudden cardiac death in Brugada syndrome. The absence of warning symptoms suggests an arrhythmic cause (e.g., ventricular fibrillation) rather than a vasovagal event. Family history and age are also important but syncope is the strongest immediate red flag.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a known drug-induced type 2 Brugada pattern (saddleback ST elevation) on ajmaline challenge is asymptomatic with no family history of sudden death. He develops a high fever due to pneumonia. A repeat ECG shows a spontaneous type 1 Brugada pattern. Which ECG finding indicates an increased risk of arrhythmic events?
questions.explanation: Fever can unmask a type 1 Brugada pattern, which significantly increases the risk of ventricular arrhythmias. A spontaneous type 1 pattern, even if transient, is a red flag for imminent danger. The other findings are not specific to Brugada syndrome.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation is started on flecainide. Two days later, she collapses and is brought to the emergency department. Her ECG shows an irregular rhythm and conduction abnormalities. Which ECG finding should raise immediate suspicion of flecainide-induced Brugada syndrome?
questions.explanation: Flecainide, a sodium channel blocker, can unmask a Brugada type 1 pattern, indicated by coved ST elevation in leads V1-V2. This finding is a red flag for life-threatening arrhythmias and requires immediate drug discontinuation. The other options are not specific to Brugada syndrome.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with Brugada syndrome and an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) presents after receiving multiple shocks. Device interrogation reveals ventricular fibrillation preceded by polymorphic VT. Which clinical finding in the history is most likely to have triggered this life-threatening arrhythmia?
questions.explanation: Fever is a well-known trigger for ventricular arrhythmias in Brugada syndrome. It can increase sodium channel inactivation, unmasking the type 1 pattern and precipitating VT/VF. Alcohol and exercise may be triggers but are less established, and beta-blockers are not first-line therapy in Brugada.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with recurrent syncope. She has a family history of sudden cardiac death in a brother aged 30. Her ECG shows a type 2 Brugada pattern (saddleback ST elevation). Which characteristic of her syncopal episodes most strongly suggests an arrhythmic cause?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal syncope (during sleep) is classic for Brugada syndrome, as arrhythmic events often occur during rest or sleep due to vagal predominance. In contrast, syncope during exercise is more typical of structural heart disease or long QT syndrome. Palpitations suggest a benign cause.
